speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My father went to the Aus-- to the American Consulate and applied-- you had to apply for a visa, and they told you you need an affidavit. So we wrote-- he promised my unc-- my great uncle-- I think he was a great uncle-- promised to find somebody to send us visas, which was a surprise to everybody, because they didn't think he was going to do it. Then, the letter came back. He said he couldn't get for all of us. It was too much. He didn't have anybody rich enough, but he could get a visa for two people and maybe my father and me would go first. And that was acceptable, so we-- we were sitting there hoping and hoping that he would send it. But somehow, it just never came until very, very late in 1937. And that's when we found out that I could get a visa immediately because of the quota system. I was born in Vienna, but he was born in what was now Romania, and Romania had used up their quota system for a year and a half. There was a strict quota system in the United States. They allowed so many people from so many countries. And apparently the Eastern countries, like Poland, Romania, they used them up because a lot of people left there. So he would have to wait a year and a half. So we were sort of stymied, and we went into 1938. Now in March, 1938, Hitler took over Austria, and I remember that day very well. I came home from something. It was already dark, late at night. And somebody yelled at me, you lousy Jew. Your day has come. And I went upstairs, and I knew right away something happened because everybody sat there crying. And we had heard that Hitler had marched into Austria. It was like a welcoming. Not one of the-- the army, nobody stopped him. Not only-- they threw bouquets at him. With open arms, he was-- you know, I mean, when they say they-- when later on, they kept on saying they were the first victim. They weren't victims. They were perfectly willing. And life became absolutely horrendous, right away, because you couldn't go on the street. They would pick up people on the street. Wherever you saw a group of people, they would pick up [INAUDIBLE], mostly-- you know, people-- businessmen, with business suits-- make them scrub the floor, the sidewalk, sometimes with a toothbrush. They made-- they took Jewish men with the beards [INAUDIBLE], they made them write on the-- on the-- on the shops, don't buy from the Jews. Or the Jews are, you know, something terrible. So it was absolutely horrible to go out. We tried not to go out. And this was in March. Now, two months later in May-- oh, my mother kept-- my mother had the best idea at that point. She said to my father, take all the money we have and go over the border. You can go to the-- you know, we all had passports in those days. We had a passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just take the money and go. He could have gone to Belgium. He could have gone to France. He could have gone anywhere because there was no such-- just go. And he said, are you crazy? I'm going to leave you and the children here alone? What are you going to do? Says, we have our relatives. Go, because something's going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't do that. I cannot le-- depart my family. And it was the biggest mistake he ever made. Because in May-- and then, one thing she did insist that he do, that he doesn't sleep at home. He slept in various places, because they used to-- right away, they went around and arrested all the men. They called it Schutzhaft, which meant they-- they arrested you for your own-- before something happens to you. For your own safety. Safety.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember it was in May. It was before my birthday. And two men in a-- he had just come home from somebody else's house and was changing his clothes when two men knocked on the door in business suits. And they said they were from the Gestapo, and they wanted to talk to my father. We knew immediately what it meant. And he said to them, can I go in the other room and change my clothes? They said, sure. They were very polite. They didn't call anybody's name. Very, very polite.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother started to cry right away. I started to cry right away. My brother just stood there forlornly. And he went and he took everything out, including his watch that he-- he had a watch that he always had in his jacket. He took everything out, left everything. And he put some old clothes on and he went with them. And that was the last time I ever saw him. And we didn't know what was going to happen. The-- the thing that they did in those days, they arrested people. Sometimes, they just took them and beat him up and let them go. Sometimes, they would take them to a concentration camp. Sometimes, they would send them someplace else. Nobody knew. You couldn't ask anybody. There was nobody would give you any answers. It was just a horror. And I-- I remember we were all crying. And I went to my Uncle Arthur's house just about a week later. He said to me, go home. Your mother wants you. So I raced home, and I didn't even take the trolley car. I-- I just ran home. And my mother had passed out on the couch. She had a piece of paper in her hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she says, they sent him to Dachau. That was-- was one of the most famous concentration camps at that time. And she would-- fainted. Why, why, why, why, why? Why didn't he leave? Why didn't he-- why didn't he listen to me? Why didn't he listen to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, before that, I forgot to tell you-- yeah. No. Before that, just before Hitler came into power, my mother tried to commit suicide twice. She didn't tell us. I never found out why. She was not well, I know that. I think she had TB, I'm not sure. But she used to go away at one point for one month to a sanatorium. And that's what happens when you have TB, you have to go away. But then one day, I must tell you this-- this was before Hitler's Anschluss-- because this is very important. It had a terrible effect on me. My father came home and he said, he sent us to-- my brother and me to various relatives to find out-- we didn't have a telephone-- whether they saw my mother. He says, she disappeared. He didn't know where she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apparently, she came home, but she had taken something. So he knew right away. They rushed her to the hospital and they pumped out her stomach. She had tried to commit suicide. Why? I will never, ever find out. She wouldn't told-- told us. And they must have told my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was because she felt she was a burden. I-- that's my imagination. She was a burden because she was sick, very sick. And she always had to go to doctor. She was on medication all the time. And she was very weak. She couldn't do very much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my grandmother was still around, and she was just terribly [INAUDIBLE]. That was in 1927. So when I asked my father, he said, we must never talk about this again. You know, just make believe it didn't happen and don't say anything to her. You'll just hurt her feelings. And when she wants to tell you, she'll tell you. She never told me. I tried to ask her, but she wouldn't tell me. And then, for a while, she was OK. But then she disappeared again, and she slit her wrists. They found her someplace and she slit her wrists. They brought her home again. And I tried to talk to her and she wouldn't talk to me. See, my mother and I didn't have a relationship. To this day, I don't know why. She wouldn't tell me this was because of this or because of that. She just wouldn't tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was terribly-- and it had such a terrible effect on me, I can't begin to tell you. Number one, I thought she was abandoning her children. I said, she has two children. What-- what-- did she ever think of us? What a selfish thing to do. So she's going to be gone and what are we going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I was just absolutely devastated. And my relationship with her was even worse after that because I was afraid to talk to her. My father said, don't you ever say anything to her. Don't you ever chastise her. Don't you ever accuse her of anything. You know, he was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very protective. And I didn't. Because there was-- you didn't, you didn't. I never found out why. So that was in the background. Then, before Hitler came to power, right before-- at the beginning of the year, my grandmother died. She had asthma, and I remember she was very ill in the house and she was coughing. And I went in because she said she wanted a glass of water. I brought her a glass of water, and she was lying there and her eyes became glassy, and I realized she died right in front of my eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when she was gone-- so when Hitler came to power, we were already without my grandmother. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and, you know-- and the one thing, probably, that my father accomplished, if he would have left, she might have committed suicide. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, I don't know why she did it. I think she was ill. She-- she was definitely ill. Something with her lungs, that's all I found out. I think she had TB, probably, because that's when you go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he was taken [INTERPOSING VOICES].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So that was just devastating to all of us. Because that meant he may be gone for years. And what happens now with the affidavit? With the thing? So immediately, we wrote to my uncle. I wrote in the name of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the United States, yeah. And explained what happened. So he said, oh, yeah. He said, well, maybe one of you should come. Maybe I should come. Oh, no. My father wrote-- she got a letter from the concentration camp. He was able to write, not very much, and whatever he wrote, there were things cut out. Because the first letter she got, that she had in her hand when she had passed out was, 'I know now why I was arrested.' And everything was cut out. There was really no reason. They just arrested all Jewish men that they could find. Either on the street or in the house. And in the house it was easy because, in those days in Europe, when you moved, you had to register with the police. You had to put your religion. So they knew where all the Jews lived that not in the ghetto, you know. So that was easy. They just went from house to house. They had all the addresses, and that's how they picked up the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was really devastating. What were we going to do? What were we going to do? And my uncle, at that point, was not very talk-- he was never very talkative. I mean, he never really held a conversation with anybody that I-- with his son, yeah. They had a good relationship.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he never had a conversation with me, except, you know, ordinary, everyday things. We never really had a conversation. I still went to work for him. And it was just-- then one day, I came-- I must-- I don't know where I came from. I went upstairs to my uncle and everybody was there, and they were crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother sort of blurted out, something awful has happened. What had happened? Kurt ist tot. Kurt is dead. Kurt was your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin. I almost passed out. I did pass out, and somebody put something under my nose. Something to smell, because I was just devastated. And they said, well, he had an accident. He ran across the street and he was hit by a car. I didn't believe it. You know, he was an athlete. He-- he skied. He did all kinds of things. He would never run across the street against-- I don't remember. He was very careful when I went with him. He was very careful. I didn't know whether to believe it or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, that was the thing. But two days later, Mitzi came. She took me on the side and she said, you know, you're old enough to know. Why did they lie to you? I don't know. Kurt committed suicide. I said, he what? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He went up to the roof of the house and he jumped down. I said, why? Did he leave a letter? No, he didn't leave a letter. He just left a note saying, give my body to a-- to a hospital for-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, they didn't do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what happened to my Uncle Arthur was really horrible. He-- it was like he was dead. He walked around like a skeleton. He was thin anyway. And he didn't eat. He ate two bites, and then would get up. He was like that, because he was the one that heard the noise, walked out, and saw his son on the street. He went downstairs and picked him up with his-- with his skull crushed.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a horrible experience. And to this day, I can't get over this. And I said, he didn't say anything to anybody? He didn't say anything to anybody. Was he depressed, Mitzi? I said, Mitzi, was he de--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He was like usual. And just all of a sudden, he-- well, I-- we all knew why he commits suicide. The young men, they picked up right away on the street. He was a nice, handsome young man. Very tall and-- and good looking. And they would put him in either concentration camps or labor camps, or stuff like that. And he just didn't want to go. And he saw no way out. See, they didn't apply to anything. Nobody-- the Jews in Vienna didn't think anything was going to happen to them. They didn't believe the stories about Hitler. They didn't believe they were going to go to concentration camps. They just didn't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So nothing was done. As far  as Arthur was concerned, he never applied for anything, or even thought about going. He was a dentist, and he had his practice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe he knew, but he didn't-- certainly didn't tell me. He didn't tell anybody. And Selma, his mother, was the first one who got over it. And she went back to her life. But I was-- could never get over it. And Arthur was horrible. He wouldn't talk. He was like living with a dead man. He never said another-- he never spoke, practically never spoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know how he spoke to his patients, but they were shrinking anyway. There weren't many left. So my mother decided, well, what were we going to do? Even though the money my parents had-- my relatives had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, this one person who-- my great aunt, who was living with her accountant husband, they raided her apartment. They came to her and they said, get out of the apartment for two days. And they took everything that they wanted to take. And then-- and then, I think he was beaten up or somebody-- we always heard how-- you didn't need the newspaper. Word of mouth, this one was beaten up, this one was gone to a concentration camp. You heard nothing but bad news all the time. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe what happened to your family next.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had to do something, so she got herself a job as a cook in somebody else's [INAUDIBLE] because at that time, Mitzi had to go, too. It was not allowed for people to have anybody-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] to have a non-Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] employee.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mitzi had to go. And some of these people that still had money still needed a cook, so my mother got herself a job as a cook. And she-- she says, that way, we get food. We get this.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was pretty feisty. She sort of picked herself up. Her mother was gone. She was the head of the household. She sort of picked herself up, and I was really amazed to see how she got herself together and took care of-- of her kids, you know. And-- so we were waiting for the affidavit. We were waiting for the affidavit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally-- oh, in the meantime though, something happened to me. My scalp itched, and when I scratched it, it was bloody. And I was losing my hair in bunches. So there were still some Jewish doctors. My mother took me to a doctor, and he said, I don't know what she has, but this is very dangerous. She's going to lose all her hair. And she's going to be very sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my thought was, if I get sick, I won't get a visa. Because before you got a visa, you had to go to the Consulate and you had to be examined by a doctor. And if there was anything wrong-- mainly, they wanted not to let people in that had TB and other things like that. But I was scared to death something was going to happen. I wasn't going to get the visa. So he said, the best thing for me to do is to put you in the hospital. So I went to a Catholic hospital. There was no [INAUDIBLE]. They took me, of course. And I was in a ward with, like, maybe 50 other people. We were all lined up in one room, a long hall and all these beds. And I remember they gave me something to put on all-- all the time. It had to be in a basin. It had to be constantly refreshed, and it has to be wet all the time. And they didn't think I was going to do that at home, so they put me there. I must say, the-- the Catholic sisters were very nice. They were not-- they were sisters, you know, and they-- they were very decent. And I had a woman next to me, she was a riot. She took a liking to me. I don't know why. I looked like a mummy, all swathed up in things and, you know, with-- with hair losing-- still coming out. Big blotches. I had a big bald spot here on one side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she says, you know? And she started to talk to me. She says, you're so educated and so cultural. I wish my son-- if you-- wish you weren't Jewish. My son would go for you. I'm looking for-- for a wife for him. Her son finally came to visit her in an SR uniform. And she introduced me, and he was very polite and talked to me. And then when he left, she says, you see? He likes you. Why do you have to be Jewish? I mean, it was really funny. I thought I looked like a mummy, you know, all dressed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the hospital for at least two weeks. And I got better, and I came out, and the doctor said, I would-- it would-- it probably would grow back. But it was a big scare to me because, first of all, I thought I was going to lose my hair. Second of all, I thought I was never going to get a visa because I wasn't going to be accepted. So then, just the waiting began. And I went to the-- the affidavit had come and had been given to the Consulate and nothing happened. There were mil-- you know, there were hundreds of them, thousands of them, I don't know. We used to stand in-- on line at night to get in in the morning. We used to go at midnight to get in at 7:00. And I used to go, and my mother was scared to death, because the Nazis would come and sort of shoot into the wings and scare everybody and yell and do-- because they were all Jews that stood on line there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, something else happened that was very horrible. In the summer, all of a sudden we heard that the council and all of the people were being recalled to the United States. We're going to get a new council. Why? Because they were selling visas. They made a nice little business there. Whoever had money was able to buy one. And who knows? I thought mine was gone, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But new council was-- apparently was better. And finally, they told me, you'll come soon. You'll come soon. Every time I got there, all you got to see was a secretary or somebody. But yet, they kept on saying, we have you on records. We have you on records. You'll get it. You'll get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it hadn't sunk into me that I was going to go alone. It hasn't sunk into me. It was just the fact that it had to go-- you had to go on. You had to go on, you know? Did you ever hear from your father who was now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was in Dachau. He was in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever get letters from him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, I have one of them here. Do you want me to read it?

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't-- you don't need to.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] but he said in there, he was sorry for me because I was all alone. This he wrote to me. My mother sent me this while I was here already. But we were able to send food packages. In those days, you could still do that. And money. But he said, don't send money. They'd take it. No-- no money. So we used to send food packages. You were able to do-- you were able to write, and once in a while, you got a letter back. Only something like that. I usually-- so we couldn't write anything and he couldn't say anything. So it was just-- all he would [INAUDIBLE] saying, I am OK. I'll survive. And don't worry about me. I'm healthy. That's all he could say. I'm OK. I will survive. And all we could say is we're OK. We're surviving. You know, nothing-- we couldn't say anything. And then, of course, the summer came and-- and all of the new things. Jews couldn't go anywhere. You couldn't go to the park. You couldn't go to my pool. I couldn't go anywhere. We couldn't go to the movies. We couldn't go to the theater. We couldn't go [INAUDIBLE]. We couldn't go anywhere. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we were sort of like prisoners, anyway. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally, I got the news that I was able to-- I was going to get an appointment for the-- I think it was in, when was it? Was it September or October? I don't remember. Probably in October. I got a notice that I should come to the consulate for my visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: The night before, I got a big sty in my eye. My eye was swollen like this. And I put compressors on it, hot and cold. And I-- I said to myself, they're going to take one look at me and they're not going to give me the visa. That was another worry. But I went there, and the doctor was fairly nice. And he looked at me. He says, oh, you have a sty. He says, that's nothing. My daughter gets them all the time. He says, use that and such and such a thing, and it will go away. And he said OK. But what I found out, that was the worst part, I needed an exit visa from the government. In other words, I needed a piece of paper that said I don't owe any taxes. I was never arrested. I don't belong to the communists, the socialists, a union, whatever. I never had any kind of a-- in my background. I was a-- I was a school girl. What could I have-- I certainly didn't owe any taxes. But this everybody had to have, or, at the border, they would send you back. That was despite the fact that Hitler always said, if only the Jews would all leave, nothing would happen to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it's not true. He wouldn't let anybody out. So what could I do? I went from office to office, you know, all of the government offices, and I asked about it. And each one sent me someplace else. If you think they're bad here, they're bad-- they're worse there. They're much worse there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they would take their time, and they're saying, well, it's-- you want it so fast. We can't do it so fast. And we have to go back into the background and we haven't done the research yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, then I went to the Jewish agencies and I explained that I had my visa and my uncle was going to send me the-- a ticket. He had sent me a ticket on-- and I had to leave in November on a boat from Le Havre, France.

speaker: interviewee
text: And boats weren't going very often, especially in the winter. You couldn't get-- you know, you couldn't say, I'll take the next one. There was no next one. I needed a certain deadline. I went to all the Jewish agencies that helped people. So I find-- I found a little-- a young man, and his name was Mr. Feder, F-E-D-E-R. I never forgot him. And he said, oh, you have everything. I'll help you. I'll try and get it for you. He said, but it will take a couple of weeks. But don't give up. We'll get it. If you have everything, there's no reason-- and you're-- you're 17. I mean, there's no reason why they should-- they're going to keep you here. You want to go. OK. I mean, you're not man. You're not anything that they want. So he says, don't worry. I'll get it for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he-- he wrote-- then he-- he wrote me a letter saying, be in such and such a building-- that was the [? Rothschild ?] building that the Nazis had taken over-- at 9:00 on this and this date. So I went out. I was there at 8:30. And as soon as I got there, there were two Nazis standing there-- soldiers-- with open bayonets. And it was closed. So I went very furtively up to the steps and I said, I have an appointment with Mr. Feder in here. No, everything's closed. [NON-ENGLISH]. That's it. So I just went down the stairs again and I stood there. And then a few other people came. They all-- he probably had other people that he had promised to get. And we had a little crowd, like maybe 10 people, maybe 12, I don't know. I didn't count.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all talking to each other, saying, Mr. Feder had promised to get you the exit visa. Yes. They're all exit visas that he had promised to get. And we were standing there. And it was a misty November day, I am quite sure. And the mist turned into rain, and we just stood there all day. We didn't go out to eat anywhere. I couldn't call my mother. We didn't have a phone. We just stood there all day. And it was raining, and we all got soaking wet. And yet, we didn't know what to do. [INAUDIBLE] they wouldn't let us in. There was no place to call. It's not like here where there are phones everywhere. And you will have to go someplace and know who to call, where to call him. We didn't. It came to be 5:00, and we were just discussing how long we should still stand there. The door opened and he came out. And he stood there. I'll never forget. He was a nice, young man. He stood out there. Oh, he wasn't that young. Anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he apologized. He said, you know, something awful happened. And therefore, it was-- what happened? It seems that in France, in Paris, a-- a Polish Jew got into the German Embassy and tried to shoot the ambassador. I don't know whether you know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all he got was a secretary. He shot somebody. So they had no-- they didn't-- they hadn't taken over France yet, so they had nothing. They couldn't do anything in France. So, but they were-- could do everything in all the other countries that they still-- they had, which was Austria and Germany and [INAUDIBLE] and all the places that they-- so they were really nasty to all the Jews. And they weren't going to let anybody out. They were going to do anything. So he came out and he explained this horrible thing happened and he was-- they stopped him from doing most everything. But he was able to get five names and five exit visas. I was the fifth one. I never forgot that. [SOBBING] Like winning the lottery for life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Like winning the lottery for life. And I went-- and I went home. My mother was hysterical. She thought definitely I'd been picked up and I disappeared. And I showed it to her. And I had just a couple of days to get ready. And we washed and ironed my clothes, and that's when, you know, she said-- she said, don't worry. I was going to take some-- she says, don't worry. I'll bring all the pictures. You don't have to worry about any of this. I'll bring everything. So I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in November of '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that night was Kristallnacht. So it was the 12th, 11th or 12th. You remember-- you hear of Kristallnacht? Crystal night? Because none of us slept because the noise was just horrendous. There was, you know, crashes and looked like-- like the city was being invaded. Only it wasn't being invaded. Everything was-- was smashed. Synagogue windows were smashed. Stores were smashed. People were arrested, and they had these horrible-- horrible Gestapo cars were, doo doo doo doo, going all night long. And you knew what they were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next morning, the city looked like it had definitely been invaded. Everything was full of glass and full of stuff all over. You couldn't walk out. You couldn't go anywhere. And I had just two days still, so I was really very much afraid to go out. At that point, I didn't go out very much. You want me to stop?
